steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims two things: (1) congestion rose over time in smaller areas, and (2) those smaller areas were less affected than very large cities throughout the period. Supporting data must confirm both the rising trend in smaller areas AND that they consistently stayed below Philadelphia.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Even though the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in the Albany-Schenectady-Troy, New York, area than in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area throughout the period...': Reverses the comparison — it says Albany was greater than Philadelphia, which contradicts the claim that smaller areas were less affected. ✗
  • Choice B — 'In 1992, the amount of traffic delay in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area was less than 30 hours per commuter per year.': Only describes one data point in Philadelphia. It says nothing about smaller areas, their relative level, or whether congestion rose. ✗
  • Choice C — 'In at least one of the three urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay was less than 20 hours per commuter per year at one point between 1990 and 2000': Notes a single low-value moment for one area but does not address the rising trend or the persistent gap between large and small cities. ✗
  • Choice D — 'While the annual number of hours of traffic delay per commuter was always lower in the Albany-Schenectady-Troy, New York, area than in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area for each year between 1990 and 2000, the amount of traffic delay rose in both areas during this period.': Confirms that Albany stayed below Philadelphia for every year (smaller areas less affected) and that both areas increased over time (congestion worsened). Both parts of the claim are supported. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
